Indian number units: 1 crore = 10 million and 1 lakh crore = 1 trillion. So ₹14,805 crore is about $1.7 billion, and ₹16.3 lakh crore of loans is roughly $195 billion.

cards.title: Bulldozers raze Abhishek Banerjee's party office in Bengal: the administration calls it an illegal building; the TMC calls it 'vengeful politics'
cards.what_happened: On Saturday afternoon, July 18, 2026, the South 24 Parganas district administration began tearing down a five-storey building used as Abhishek Banerjee's party office in Amtala, saying it had been constructed illegally without a sanctioned building plan and in breach of building rules. Officials said notices were served on July 15 asking those responsible to appear at a hearing, and that they moved in with bulldozers only after two notices went unanswered. The demolition was carried out under local revenue and block officials and escorted by state police and central armed police forces. TMC supporters gathered at the site and the party denounced the action as 'vengeful politics,' while BJP leaders described it as lawful administrative enforcement.
key_points: Five-storey building used as Abhishek Banerjee's Amtala office demolished Saturday
District administration says it was built illegally, without a sanctioned plan
Notices served July 15; bulldozers moved in when they went unanswered
TMC cries 'vengeful politics'; BJP calls it lawful action — polls loom in Bengal
cards.moot: Demolishing an 'illegal' building can be a genuine enforcement of the law or a tool of political pressure — and often the same act is claimed to be both. That is precisely why courts insist on due process, a proper notice and a hearing, before a bulldozer moves: the safeguard, not the demolition itself, is what separates rule of law from rule by force.

depth.background: 'Bulldozer politics' — where authorities demolish the property of people they accuse of wrongdoing, often political opponents — has become a charged tactic across India, so much so that the Supreme Court in 2024 laid down binding rules requiring advance notice and a proper hearing before any demolition. Abhishek Banerjee is a Lok Sabha member (an elected MP) and the general secretary — the de facto number two — of the Trinamool Congress (TMC), the party that governs West Bengal under his aunt, Chief Minister Mamata Banerjee. Amtala sits in South 24 Parganas district, on the outskirts of Kolkata.
depth.why_it_matters: This is a rare case of a 'bulldozer' demolition aimed squarely at the office of one of the country's most powerful opposition figures — and, unusually, carried out by the administration of a state his own party runs, framed as a routine building-violation action. It tests whether demolition powers are being applied even-handedly or wielded as a political weapon, and it lands with West Bengal's assembly elections due in 2026, cranking up the already-bitter contest between the TMC and the BJP-led central government.
depth.ripple_effects: Expect a political and likely legal fight: the TMC may challenge the demolition in court, invoking the Supreme Court's due-process rules, while the BJP will argue no one is above building law. The episode feeds the wider national debate over 'bulldozer justice' and whether officials are honouring the notice-and-hearing safeguards the top court ordered. And it heats up an already-tense Bengal ahead of state polls, where every such flashpoint becomes campaign ammunition.
depth.why_now: The administration says it acted only after the July 15 notices drew no response — but the timing, targeting a high-profile TMC leader's office months before Bengal's assembly elections, is what has turned a building-code case into a national political flashpoint.
depth.watch_next: Whether the TMC takes the demolition to court, and whether the Supreme Court's demolition guidelines — proper notice, a hearing, and time to respond — are found to have been followed.

key_terms.definition: The practice of demolishing people's buildings as punishment or pressure, often against opponents; the Supreme Court has ruled it can only be done with due process.
The regional party that governs West Bengal, led by Chief Minister Mamata Banerjee; Abhishek Banerjee is her nephew and the party's second-in-command.
A directly elected member of the Lok Sabha, the lower house of India's Parliament; Abhishek Banerjee holds one such seat.
The official permission a local authority must grant before a structure is built; construction without it can be declared illegal and ordered demolished.

cards.title: Axis Bank's profit jumps 23% to ₹7,114 crore and bad loans shrink — a reassuring quarter from one of India's biggest private banks
cards.what_happened: On July 18, 2026, Axis Bank reported that its standalone net profit for the June quarter rose about 23% from a year earlier to ₹7,114 crore, ahead of analysts' expectations. Its net interest income (NII) — the core profit from lending, i.e. the gap between what it earns on loans and pays out on deposits — climbed 8% to ₹14,646 crore, while its net interest margin (NIM), that gap expressed as a percentage, stood at 3.46%. Crucially, asset quality improved: gross non-performing assets (loans unlikely to be repaid) fell to 1.28% of total loans from 1.57% a year earlier, and net NPAs eased to 0.39%. The bank's overall balance sheet grew 20% to roughly ₹19.2 lakh crore (about ₹19.2 trillion).
key_points: Standalone net profit rose 23% year-on-year to ₹7,114 crore, beating forecasts
Net interest income up 8% to ₹14,646 crore; margin (NIM) at 3.46%
Bad loans (gross NPAs) fell to 1.28% from 1.57% a year earlier
Balance sheet grew 20% to about ₹19.2 lakh crore
cards.moot: A bank's profit is only half the story — the other half is the quality of its loans. Rising earnings paired with shrinking bad loans is the healthy combination, because a bank can always boost profits temporarily by lending recklessly, only to pay for it later when those loans go bad. Watching non-performing assets alongside profit tells you whether the growth is real and durable.

depth.background: Axis Bank is one of India's largest private-sector banks, and its quarterly results are watched as a health-check on both the bank and the wider economy — because banks make money by lending, their profits rise and fall with how much people and businesses are borrowing and how reliably they repay. Every three months (a 'quarter') listed companies publish their financial scorecard; the April–June period is 'Q1' of India's financial year. Investors zero in on a handful of numbers: how much the bank earned, how fat its lending margin is, and how many loans are going sour — the last being the classic warning light for any bank.
depth.why_it_matters: A big private bank posting double-digit profit growth while its bad loans shrink is a good sign for India's financial system — it suggests borrowers are largely keeping up with repayments even as global risks (a spiking oil price, a weak rupee, inflation just above the RBI's target) cloud the outlook. Bank results also move the market heavily because lenders are among the largest weights in India's Sensex and Nifty indices, so a strong showing from a heavyweight like Axis can lift sentiment across the board.
depth.ripple_effects: Steady margins and falling bad loans give the bank room to keep lending, which supports credit flowing to homebuyers, businesses and consumers. Strong results from Axis raise the bar and set the tone for peers like HDFC Bank, ICICI and Kotak reporting around the same time, shaping how investors judge the whole banking sector this earnings season. But the numbers also carry a caution flag for the quarters ahead: with inflation now above the Reserve Bank of India's comfort zone and the RBI governor flagging the West Asia (Middle East) conflict and an uncertain monsoon as growth risks, banks' margins could get squeezed if interest rates or deposit costs shift.
depth.why_now: This is a scheduled quarterly earnings release, the first of India's 2026-27 financial year — the standard moment banks open their books. Its significance is sharpened by the backdrop: it lands the same week India's inflation breached the RBI's 4% target and the rupee stayed under pressure from higher oil prices, making a clean, profitable quarter a welcome data point.
depth.watch_next: How rival private banks (HDFC Bank, ICICI, Kotak) fare in their Q1 results, and whether banks' lending margins hold up if the RBI shifts course on interest rates amid rising inflation.
depth.market_link: Moves Axis Bank (AXISBANK) shares and sets the tone for India's bank-heavy Nifty and Sensex; a read-through for private-sector peers HDFC Bank, ICICI Bank and Kotak Mahindra reporting this earnings season, and a gauge of Indian credit health as inflation runs above the RBI's target.

key_terms.definition: A bank's core earnings from lending — the difference between the interest it collects on loans and the interest it pays on deposits.
That lending profit expressed as a percentage of the bank's assets; a higher NIM means the bank keeps more from each rupee it lends. Axis reported 3.46%.
A loan the borrower has stopped repaying, so the bank may never get its money back. A falling NPA ratio means loan quality is improving.
A three-month slice of the financial year; Q1 in India covers April–June. Companies report results each quarter as a running scorecard.

cards.title: Police forcibly move a fasting Sonam Wangchuk to hospital on day 21 of his Ladakh protest
cards.what_happened: In the early hours of Saturday, day 21 of his indefinite fast at Delhi's Jantar Mantar protest site, Delhi Police moved Wangchuk to Safdarjung Hospital, citing a Delhi High Court order and medical advice; hospital bulletins and an independent AIIMS expert said he needed immediate care. Protesters said white bedsheets were used to shield the operation, and that he was taken away in about five minutes. Wangchuk reportedly refused medical interventions, and fellow activists — including at least 21 others still fasting — vowed to continue; one, Abhijeet Dipke, began a fresh fast in his place.
key_points: Climate activist removed from Delhi's Jantar Mantar before dawn
He was 20+ days into a hunger strike for Ladakh's rights
Doctors said he needed 'immediate medical intervention'
Opposition calls it repression of peaceful protest
cards.moot: How a government responds to a hunger strike — negotiate, ignore, or forcibly end it — often becomes a bigger story than the original demand, because the method of dissent tests the state's tolerance for protest.

depth.background: Sonam Wangchuk is a famous engineer-educator from Ladakh, the high-altitude region in India's far north that was carved out as a federally-run 'union territory' in 2019 (meaning New Delhi, not a local elected government, runs it). Since then, Ladakhi groups have demanded statehood and protection under the Constitution's Sixth Schedule — a special provision that gives tribal areas control over their own land, culture and resources. Wangchuk has led repeated hunger strikes to push these demands, and this time also folded in a nationwide student protest over leaked exam papers.
depth.why_it_matters: This turns a regional grievance into a national test of how India handles peaceful dissent. Wangchuk is a globally respected figure, so images of police carrying away a frail, fasting protester draw scrutiny at home and abroad. It also keeps a spotlight on Ladakh's unresolved constitutional demands and on whether the central government negotiates or simply waits protests out.
depth.ripple_effects: Opposition and Left parties have condemned the removal and backed the protest, sharpening the fight just before Parliament's Monsoon Session opens on Monday. If Wangchuk's health worsens, or if the promised July 20 march to Parliament goes ahead, tensions in Ladakh — where similar protests turned violent before — could flare again, pressuring the government to reopen talks on statehood and Sixth Schedule protections.
depth.why_now: The fast had reached a medically dangerous stage on day 21, and a court order plus hospital warnings gave authorities the legal cover to act — timed, protesters allege, to the pre-dawn lull when fewer supporters and cameras were present.
depth.watch_next: Whether Wangchuk resumes or ends the fast in hospital, and if the July 20 'march to Parliament' proceeds as the Monsoon Session begins.

key_terms.definition: A part of India's Constitution that lets certain tribal areas run their own councils with power over land, forests and local customs.
A region governed directly by the central government in New Delhi rather than by its own fully-empowered state government.

cards.title: US senators push a bill to slap 100% tariffs on India for buying Russian oil — New Delhi says it's 'closely following'
cards.what_happened: A bipartisan group of more than 60 US senators is backing the 'Senator Lindsey O. Graham Sanctioning Russia Act of 2026', introduced by Senator Richard Blumenthal alongside Graham's widow, Darline Graham. The revised bill would let the US president impose tariffs of up to 100% on imports from the five biggest purchasers of Russian oil and gas — India, China, Slovakia, Hungary and Azerbaijan — while carving out an exception for countries that import less than 15% of Russia's gas and are cutting back. It scales down an earlier, blunter proposal of 500% tariffs and gives the president discretion to waive the penalties in the US national interest; it has the backing of Senate Majority Leader John Thune and President Trump. At its July 17 briefing, India's Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) spokesperson Randhir Jaiswal said New Delhi is 'closely following' the legislation, adding that India's oil purchases are guided by its energy-security needs and that it sources crude from many suppliers.
key_points: Bipartisan US bill would let Trump hit India with up to 100% tariffs
Targets the top 5 buyers of Russian oil: India, China, Slovakia, Hungary, Azerbaijan
60+ senators back it and President Trump supports it
India's foreign ministry: 'closely following', cites its energy-security needs
cards.moot: Modern economic pressure increasingly works through third parties: rather than only sanctioning an adversary directly, powers threaten that country's trading partners, turning them into pressure points to isolate the target. It's also a reminder that cheap energy can carry a hidden geopolitical price — a bargain barrel today can become a diplomatic liability tomorrow.

depth.background: When Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022, the West hit Moscow with sanctions and largely stopped buying its oil, so Russia was forced to sell its crude at a steep discount. India — which imports most of the oil it burns and had bought almost none from Russia before the war — jumped at the cheap barrels, and Russian crude has since grown to roughly a third of India's oil imports, making India one of the world's biggest buyers. Western governments see that flow of oil money as funding Russia's war, and have been looking for ways to choke it off — including 'secondary' measures that punish not Russia itself, but the third countries still buying its oil. This bill is the latest, and it is named after the late US Senator Lindsey Graham, a long-time champion of tough Russia sanctions.
depth.why_it_matters: The United States is one of India's largest export markets, so a 100% tariff would roughly double the price of Indian goods sold in America — a potential body-blow to Indian exporters of everything from smartphones and machinery to textiles, gems and generic medicines. In effect, Washington is threatening India's access to the US market to force it to give up cheap Russian oil, putting New Delhi in a bind between two things it values: discounted energy that keeps fuel and inflation in check, and a booming trade relationship with the US.
depth.ripple_effects: The threat lands right in the middle of delicate India-US trade talks and gives Washington fresh leverage. If India were pushed to buy less Russian crude, it would likely pay more for oil elsewhere — swelling its import bill, pressuring the rupee, and adding to inflation that has already pushed past the Reserve Bank of India's comfort zone. Indian refiners such as Reliance, IOC, BPCL and HPCL, which have leaned heavily on cheap Russian barrels, would see margins squeezed. But this is still a bill, not a law: it must pass both chambers of Congress and be signed, and even then the president could waive the tariffs — so for now it works as pressure and a bargaining chip as much as a firm penalty.
depth.why_now: The measure has gathered momentum as the Ukraine war grinds on and crude prices spike amid the US-Iran conflict at the Strait of Hormuz, sharpening Western resolve to cut Russia's oil revenue. It crossed the 60-cosponsor mark this week, and India's MEA was pressed on it at its July 17 media briefing — putting the threat squarely on New Delhi's radar.
depth.watch_next: Whether the bill gets a full Senate floor vote, and any signal from Trump on whether he would actually use — or waive — the tariff power against India.
depth.market_link: A risk for Indian exporters (IT services, pharma, autos, gems & jewellery, textiles) and for refiners that process Russian crude such as Reliance (RELIANCE), IOC, BPCL and HPCL; pressure on the rupee (USDINR), and broadly supportive of global oil prices if big buyers are forced to shift away from Russian barrels.

key_terms.definition: Penalties aimed not at the sanctioned country itself (Russia) but at third countries that keep trading with it — here, taxing imports from nations that buy Russian oil.
India's foreign ministry, which handles the country's diplomacy and speaks for the government on international issues.
A lawmaker who formally adds their name in support of a bill; a high count (here 60+) signals it has broad backing and a better chance of passing.
Oil Russia sells below world prices because Western buyers shun it; India buying it cheaply is what makes it a target of this bill.

cards.title: Gujarat is India's most investment-friendly state: NITI Aayog's first-ever ranking scores all 36 states and UTs — Maharashtra and Tamil Nadu follow
cards.what_happened: On Friday, July 17, 2026, NITI Aayog released the first-ever Investment Friendliness Index (IFI) 2026 in New Delhi, ranking all 28 states and 8 union territories on how attractive they are to investors. Among large states, Gujarat came first with 56.6 out of 100, followed by Maharashtra (53.7) and Tamil Nadu (53.3); Uttarakhand, Assam and Himachal Pradesh led the hilly and north-eastern group, while Goa, Delhi and Chandigarh topped the union territories and city-states. The index scores each place across eight 'pillars', weighted by importance: infrastructure (25%), business climate (20%), resources (15%), regulatory ease (12%), government policy (10%), financial health (7%), institutional environment (6%) and environmental resilience (5%). It drew on a survey of more than 1,850 investors plus 165 stakeholder inputs, and sorted states into bands — 'top performers', 'frontrunners', 'emerging performers' and 'aspiring' — so laggards can see exactly where they trail. NITI Aayog Vice Chairman Ashok Kumar Lahiri framed it plainly: 'It's an exercise to tell the states where they're doing well, where they're not.'
key_points: Gujarat tops with 56.6/100; Maharashtra (53.7) and Tamil Nadu (53.3) next
First edition of NITI Aayog's Investment Friendliness Index (IFI) 2026
Scores all 28 states + 8 UTs on eight pillars, from infrastructure to green resilience
Meant to spark 'competitive federalism' — states racing to attract private money
cards.moot: Governments increasingly use public rankings as a low-cost tool of reform: turning performance into a visible league table creates competition and political pressure to improve without spending a rupee. But such indices also tend to reward places that are already ahead, which can quietly deepen the gap between richer and poorer regions.

depth.background: NITI Aayog is the Indian government's main policy think-tank — it advises the Centre and the states and designs reform ideas (its name means 'policy' and stands for National Institution for Transforming India). One of its favourite tools is the 'ranking index': it scores every state on some goal and publishes a league table, betting that no chief minister wants to sit at the bottom, so the rankings push states to compete and improve. This is the logic of 'competitive federalism' — the Centre nudging states to out-reform each other. India badly wants more private investment (money that companies pour into factories, ports and jobs) to hit its stated goal of becoming a developed nation, 'Viksit Bharat', by 2047, and where that investment lands depends heavily on how easy each state makes it to do business.
depth.why_it_matters: Investment is the fuel for jobs, factories and incomes, and in India it is the states — not just the Centre — that control the land, power, permits and local rules that decide whether a company builds there or elsewhere. By turning that into a public scorecard, NITI Aayog is trying to shift the burden of reform onto states and shame or spur them into fixing the bottlenecks (slow approvals, patchy infrastructure) that scare investors off. For readers, the ranking is a rough map of where India's private money is likely to flow next — and why Gujarat and Maharashtra keep pulling ahead while others fall behind.
depth.ripple_effects: Top-ranked states like Gujarat can use the badge to court investors and defend their pro-business reputation, while lower-ranked ones face pressure to copy what the leaders do well — Gujarat's ports and power, Maharashtra's pull for venture capital, Tamil Nadu's infrastructure and exports. Over time, indices like this tend to concentrate investment in the states that already lead, potentially widening the gap between richer western/southern states and poorer eastern ones — a live political issue given India's north-south tensions. It also feeds the Centre-state relationship: states may lobby for a bigger say (and more central funds) based on where they land, and the pillar on 'environmental resilience' signals that green risk is now part of how investment-worthiness is judged.
depth.why_now: The launch is a deliberate policy event, timed as India pushes its 'Viksit Bharat by 2047' drive and leans on private investment to sustain growth amid global headwinds — a weak-ish rupee, costly oil from the Iran conflict, and China's grip on supply chains. Publishing a first-of-its-kind ranking now is meant to jolt states into the reform race at a moment the country wants capital flowing in.
depth.watch_next: Whether lagging states announce reforms in response, and how the rankings shift in future editions — plus whether the Centre ties any funding or incentives to a state's IFI score.
depth.market_link: Not a direct market mover, but a signal of where India's capital expenditure may concentrate — supportive read-through over time for infrastructure, ports, power and industrial names tied to leading states like Gujarat, Maharashtra and Tamil Nadu.

key_terms.definition: The Indian government's top policy think-tank, which advises the Centre and states and publishes ranking indices to push reform.
The idea of getting India's states to compete against each other on reforms and outcomes, so public league tables become a spur to do better.
A region governed more directly by the Centre than a full state (e.g. Delhi, Chandigarh); the index ranks UTs and city-states in their own group.
India's official goal of becoming a fully developed nation by 2047, the 100th year of independence — the backdrop for the drive to attract more investment.

cards.title: India clears Wegovy to treat fatty-liver disease — the first weight-loss jab approved for a silent condition that afflicts nearly 4 in 10 Indian adults
cards.what_happened: On July 17, 2026, the CDSCO approved Wegovy (semaglutide 2.4 mg injection) for treating non-cirrhotic MASH — fatty-liver disease that has caused moderate-to-advanced liver scarring but not yet full cirrhosis — in adults, used alongside a reduced-calorie diet and more exercise. The nod was based on the global 'ESSENCE' clinical trial, in which about 63% of patients on the drug saw their liver inflammation (steatohepatitis) resolve and roughly 37% saw their liver fibrosis (scarring) improve. Novo Nordisk says it is the first and only GLP-1 drug approved for this liver use, in India and globally.
key_points: India's drug regulator CDSCO approves Novo Nordisk's Wegovy for serious fatty-liver disease (MASH)
First GLP-1 'weight-loss' injection cleared for a liver condition in India
Trial: 63% of patients saw liver inflammation resolve; 37% saw scarring improve
Studies suggest ~39% of Indian adults have fatty liver; the drug is already sold here for weight loss
cards.moot: A medicine's value often expands far beyond the problem it was built for. Because obesity, type-2 diabetes and fatty-liver disease share the same metabolic roots, a single drug designed to curb appetite can end up treating a whole cluster of linked conditions — which is why one class of injections is quietly reshaping the treatment of several of the world's biggest chronic diseases at once.

depth.background: Fatty liver disease is exactly what it sounds like — fat building up inside the liver. In its harmful form (now called MASLD, and its more dangerous, inflamed stage MASH) it can quietly scar the organ over years, leading to cirrhosis, liver failure or cancer, often with no symptoms until late. It is exploding in India alongside rising obesity, diabetes and sedentary, high-sugar diets: recent screening studies suggest around 39% of Indian adults — nearly four in ten — already have it, yet until recently there was no approved medicine, only 'eat less, move more'. Wegovy is the branded version of semaglutide, the blockbuster 'GLP-1' drug (the same molecule as Ozempic) that mimics a gut hormone to curb appetite and was first cleared in India in 2025 for weight loss. India's Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO) is the national regulator that decides which medicines can be sold — the equivalent of the US FDA.
depth.why_it_matters: Fatty liver is a large, largely invisible epidemic in India, and until now doctors had no dedicated, proven drug for it — the appetite-suppressing jab many Indians already know as a weight-loss shot now doubles as a liver treatment. It extends the 'GLP-1 revolution' beyond diabetes and obesity into organ disease, offering millions at risk of long-term liver damage an actual medical option rather than lifestyle advice alone. Because the liver damage it targets is a leading path to cirrhosis and transplants, catching and treating it earlier could ease a heavy future burden on India's health system.
depth.ripple_effects: Expect a fresh wave of demand — and a sharpening price fight. Novo Nordisk cut Wegovy's India price by up to 48% in 2026 (a starting dose now runs from roughly ₹5,660 a month) as Sun Pharma and others launched far cheaper generic semaglutide (as low as ~₹3,400 a month), so a new, serious medical indication could widen the market while intensifying that competition. It may push more Indians and doctors to screen for fatty liver, since there is now something to prescribe. Affordability and whether insurers cover it will decide how many actually benefit — at a few thousand rupees a month, it remains out of reach for many. And it deepens India's tie to the global obesity-drug boom now reshaping big pharma.
depth.why_now: The approval follows results from the international ESSENCE trial that established semaglutide can heal the inflamed, scarring form of fatty liver, and it comes as the CDSCO clears the indication in India — turning trial data into a treatment doctors can actually prescribe. It also lands amid a rush of cheap semaglutide launches in India this year, making the liver market newly worth fighting for.
depth.watch_next: Whether cheaper generic semaglutide also wins the fatty-liver indication, how insurers price and cover the treatment, and how quickly Indian doctors start screening for and prescribing against MASH.
depth.market_link: Positive for Novo Nordisk (NVO), which makes Wegovy; adds competitive pressure in India's fast-growing anti-obesity market where Sun Pharma and other domestic firms sell cheap generic semaglutide; a read-through for the global GLP-1 drug boom.

key_terms.definition: Fat building up in the liver (MASLD); its inflamed, scarring stage (MASH) can progress to cirrhosis, liver failure or cancer, often silently. Increasingly common in India.
A class of drug (semaglutide/Wegovy/Ozempic) that copies a gut hormone to reduce appetite and blood sugar; originally for diabetes and weight loss, now expanding to other diseases.
The Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation — India's national drug regulator that approves whether medicines are safe and effective to sell, like the US FDA.
Scarring of an organ's tissue; in the liver, advancing fibrosis is the dangerous step on the road to cirrhosis and organ failure.

cards.title: Reliance's profit 'falls 25%' — but only because of a one-off a year ago: revenue still jumped 25% as oil, Jio and retail powered India's biggest company
cards.what_happened: On July 17, 2026, RIL reported results for Q1 FY27 (the April–June quarter). Consolidated net profit — the group's total bottom-line earnings — came in around ₹23,001 crore, down roughly 25% from about ₹30,681 crore a year earlier (the portion attributable to RIL's own shareholders was about ₹20,946 crore). But that headline fall is misleading: revenue actually rose 25.4% to ₹3,11,850 crore, and operating profit (EBITDA — earnings before interest, tax and accounting write-downs, a cleaner gauge of the core business) climbed about 10% to ₹51,403 crore. The reason profit 'fell' is a base effect: the year-ago quarter was flattered by a one-time ₹8,924 crore gain from selling listed investments, which won't repeat. Strip that out and underlying earnings grew, led by a ~30% jump in the oil-to-chemicals division.
key_points: Q1 net profit about ₹23,001 crore, down ~25% from a year earlier
But revenue rose 25% to ₹3.12 lakh crore — the business actually grew
The 'drop' is a base effect: last year's quarter had a ₹8,924 crore one-off gain
Oil-to-chemicals sales up ~30%; operating profit (EBITDA) up ~10%
cards.moot: A falling profit number isn't always bad news — you have to compare like with like. One-time gains can inflate a past quarter and make normal, healthy growth look like a decline. When a headline profit and the revenue trend point in opposite directions, the revenue and operating-profit (EBITDA) lines usually tell the truer story of whether a business is actually growing.

depth.background: Reliance Industries (RIL) is India's most valuable company and its results move the whole stock market, because it is the single biggest weight in the Sensex and Nifty (the two headline indices that track India's top companies). RIL is really three businesses in one: 'O2C' — oil refining and petrochemicals, turning crude into fuels and plastics; Jio, the country's largest mobile-phone and broadband network; and Reliance Retail, its huge chain of stores. Every quarter (a three-month reporting period) it publishes its profit and sales, and investors comb through them for the health of the Indian consumer, telecom and energy.
depth.why_it_matters: Because RIL is so large, its results tug the entire index — so how the market reads them affects millions of investors' portfolios and mutual funds. Just as important, this is a textbook lesson in not trusting a scary headline number: a '25% profit crash' actually hides a business that grew its sales and operating profit by double digits. The dip is an accounting mirage created by a one-off in the comparison quarter, not a sign the company is shrinking.
depth.ripple_effects: For the Sensex and Nifty, a clean underlying quarter from their heaviest stock is reassuring even if the headline looks ugly. For consumers and rivals, strong Jio and retail numbers signal Indians are still spending on data and shopping. The O2C surge ties RIL's fortunes to global oil — and with crude spiking on the US–Iran war at the Strait of Hormuz, refining margins are in focus. And investors will keep hunting for clues on the long-awaited stock-market listings (IPOs) of Jio and Reliance Retail, which could unlock huge value.
depth.why_now: This is a scheduled quarterly earnings release, but it lands at a charged moment: oil prices are elevated because of the Iran conflict (which flows straight into RIL's refining business), and Indian markets are jittery after inflation breached the RBI's target and the rupee weakened past 96 — so a bellwether like RIL is watched even more closely.
depth.watch_next: Management's commentary on Jio tariff hikes, retail expansion and any timeline for the Jio and Reliance Retail IPOs — plus how refining margins hold up while oil stays volatile.
depth.market_link: Directly moves Reliance Industries (RIL) — the heaviest stock in the Nifty 50 and Sensex, so it tugs both indices; a read-through for refiners and petrochemicals (oil prices), telecom (Jio) and organised retail.

key_terms.definition: The total bottom-line earnings of a company and all the businesses it controls, added together — RIL's covers O2C, Jio and Retail as one group.
A profit from a one-time event — here, selling investments — that boosts a single quarter but won't recur, so it distorts year-on-year comparisons.
When a change looks big or small mainly because of an unusual figure in the earlier period you're comparing against, not because of what happened now.
Earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation — a measure of operating profit that strips out financing and accounting effects to show how the core business is doing.

cards.title: Antonelli beats Verstappen to pole at the Belgian GP as Mercedes keeps dominating qualifying
cards.what_happened: At the Belgian Grand Prix at Spa-Francorchamps, Antonelli clinched his sixth pole position of the season, edging four-time champion Max Verstappen by 0.317 seconds. Verstappen admitted he only reached the front row thanks to a 'tow' — a slipstream — from Red Bull team-mate Isack Hadjar, who runs at the back after engine-change penalties. Reigning champion Lando Norris will drop 10 places on the grid for using too many engine parts, and Lewis Hamilton crashed his Ferrari in final practice earlier in the day.
key_points: Kimi Antonelli takes his 6th pole of 2026
Beat Max Verstappen by 0.317s at Spa
Verstappen aided by a team-mate's slipstream
Champion Lando Norris hit with a 10-place grid penalty
cards.moot: Even in a sport built on individual speed, small tactics like slipstreaming and the rules on engine parts can decide who starts where — proving strategy and reliability matter as much as raw pace.

depth.background: Formula 1's qualifying decides the starting order for Sunday's race, and starting up front is a big advantage on tracks where overtaking is hard. Italian teenager Kimi Antonelli, in only his second F1 season with Mercedes, leads the 2026 drivers' championship, and Mercedes cars have been fastest over one lap all year.
depth.why_it_matters: It reinforces Antonelli as the man to beat in a breakout season and tightens Mercedes' grip on qualifying. Grid penalties for Norris and others reshuffle the order, giving Antonelli a strong chance to convert pole into points and extend his title lead.
depth.ripple_effects: Track position at Spa can decide the race, so this hands Antonelli momentum in the championship; Norris's penalty forces a recovery drive; and Verstappen's reliance on a team-mate's tow hints at Red Bull's pace deficit. Rivals will study Mercedes' one-lap edge before the next round.
depth.why_now: Saturday qualifying set the grid for Sunday's Belgian Grand Prix.
depth.watch_next: Sunday's race — whether Antonelli converts pole into a win, and how far Norris climbs from his penalty.

key_terms.definition: The first place on the starting grid, earned by the fastest lap in qualifying.
Following closely behind another car to sit in its wake, cutting air resistance and gaining speed.

cards.title: India vs England ODI series goes to a Lord's decider — with Rohit Sharma's future in the spotlight
cards.what_happened: England levelled the series 1-1 by winning the second ODI in Cardiff, guided by Joe Root's unbeaten 99. That sets up a winner-takes-all third ODI at Lord's, cricket's most famous ground. Amid swirling retirement rumours, Rohit turned up for India's optional practice and looked in good touch, while India named all-rounder Harsh Dubey to replace the injured Washington Sundar, opening the door for spinner Kuldeep Yadav's possible return. India have not won a bilateral ODI series in England since 2018.
key_points: Series level 1-1 after England win in Cardiff
Decider at Lord's; India seek first series win in England since 2018
Rohit Sharma under retirement speculation, joins optional practice
Harsh Dubey replaces injured Washington Sundar
cards.moot: In sport, individual and team stories often collide in one game — pressure on an ageing star can either sharpen a career-defining performance or hasten its end.

depth.background: India and England are playing a three-match one-day international (ODI) series — the 50-overs-a-side format. Veteran opener Rohit Sharma, 39, was recently removed as India's ODI captain and has struggled for runs, fuelling talk that this could be one of his last outings and that younger opener Yashasvi Jaiswal is ready to replace him.
depth.why_it_matters: It's a genuine decider with a personal subplot: a legend of Indian cricket may be playing to prove he still belongs, in the same match that decides the series. For India, ending a long drought of ODI series wins in England would be a marker of the team's transition to a younger core.
depth.ripple_effects: Rohit's performance could shape selection debates and his own career timeline heading toward major tournaments; a series win or loss feeds into how India rebuilds its one-day side. For England, backing up their Cardiff comeback would validate a reshaped ODI unit.
depth.why_now: The Cardiff result squared the series at 1-1, so everything rests on the final match at Lord's this weekend.
depth.watch_next: The result of the Lord's decider — and whether Rohit's showing quiets or amplifies the retirement talk.

key_terms.definition: A limited-overs cricket match where each side bats for 50 overs, usually finished in a single day.
A series played between just two nations, separate from multi-team tournaments like the World Cup.
